Facts.text: St Hormisdas elected as Pope succeeding Pope Sympowerus
Henry I succeeds father Robert II as King of the Franks (1031-60)
Patriarch of Constantinople Michael Caerularius, as head of a Synod held in Constantinople excommunicates Cardinal Humbert of the Western church (Rome)
French viceroy of Naples Montpensier surrenders
Emperor Rudolf II grants Silezische protestants freedom of religion
Gerardus Vossius resigns as Dutch regent States college leader
The Riot Act takes effect in Great Britain
North America: French explorer Pierre Gaultier de Varennes, sieur de La Vérendrye reaches the western shore of Lake Michigan
Earl of Chesterfield says "Idleness is only refuge of weak minds"
Scottish settlers arrive at Pictou, Nova Scotia (Canada)
Elisha Brown Jr presses a 1,235-pound cheese ball at his farm
Citizens of Bogotá, New Granada (now Colombia), declare independence from Spain
Java War begins in the Dutch East Indies, a rebellion against Dutch rule led by Javanese Prince Diponegoro - lasts till 1830 and kills over 200,000 Javanese
German astronomer Theodor discovers Comet Brorsen-Metcalf
Start of 1st Lancashire-Yorkshire clash at Hyde Park, Sheffield
1st train from Rotterdam to Utrecht in Netherlands
Fee first charged to see a baseball game (50 cents) (NY beats Brooklyn 22-18)
Confederate states' congress began holding sessions in Richmond, Virginia
Guerrilla campaign in Georgia (Porter's & Poindexter's) [->SEP 20]
Battle at Stephenson's Depot, Virginia: 200 killed or injured
Battle of Peachtree Creek-Atlanta Campaign
Sea battle of Lissa-Austria vs Italy
First use of tax stamps on cigarettes
Children's Hospital Boston is founded by Dr. Francis Henry Brown and other Harvard Medical School graduates, as a 20-bed facility in the South End of Boston, Massachusetts
British Columbia joins the confederation of Canada
First US intercollegiate track meet is held in Saratoga, NY; Princeton wins

Great Railroad Strike: Labor issues turn violent as state militia confronts much larger crowd of rail workers and supporters - rocks met with bullets in Baltimore, Maryland; 10 killed and federal troops called in to restore order
First telephone introduced in Hawaii
Swedish expedition headed by Nils Adolf Erik Nordenskiöld, aboard SS Vega, becomes the first to navigate the Northeast passage
"Gibbons Stamp Monthly" begins publishing
Snow and hail in Calais, Maine
2000 fed troops recalled from Chicago, having ended Pullman strike
Morocco signs an agreement with France fixing Morocco's frontier with Algeria, a French colony
Brooklyn Superbas pitcher Mal Eason no-hits St Louis Cardinals, 2-0 at Robison Field, St. Louis
A train wreck on the Pere Marquette Railroad near Salem, Michigan kills thirty and injures seventy more
Boston Red Sox Smokey Joe Wood no-hits St Louis Browns, 5-0
Generals Henry Wilson/Auguste Dubail sign plan for British Expeditionary army in case of war with Germany
Phillies Sherry Magee steals home twice in 1 game
Turkish troops take Adrianopel & Erdine from Bulgaria
Armed resistance against British rule begins in Ulster
Pact of Corfu signed: Serbs, Croats & Slovenes form Yugoslavia
WW I draft lottery held; #258 is 1st drawn
Dutch soccer club Sportclub Heerenveen is formed; initially known as Athleta; KNVB Cup Champions 2008–09
French and British Togoland make separate mandates within the League of Nations
New York Yankees hit into a triple-play, but beat Philadelphia A's 9-2
Federation Internationale des Echecs (FIDE) forms in Paris
Tehran, Persia comes under martial law after the American vice consul, Robert Imbrie, is killed by a religious mob enraged by rumors he had poisoned a fountain and killed several people
Beirut sultan Pasja al-Atrasj calls Druzen for holy war against France
Treaty of Nettuno between Italy and the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes (later Yugoslavia) is signed but is only ratified in 1928
A convention of the Methodist Church votes to allow women to become priests
The government of Hungary issues a decree ordering Gypsies to end their nomadic ways, settle permanently in one place, subject themselves to the same laws and taxes as other Hungarians
106°F (41°C), Washington, D.C. (district record)
In Washington, D.C., police fire tear gas on World War I veterans part of the Bonus Expeditionary Force who attempt to march to the White House
Germany: Two-hundred Jewish merchants are arrested in Nuremberg and paraded through the streets
In London, 500,000 march against anti-Semitism
118°F (48°C), Keokuk, Iowa (state record)
"Gang Busters" premieres on NBC radio; runs for 21 years until November 27, 1957
A Royal Dutch Airlines plane en route from Milan to Frankfurt crashes into a Swiss mountain, killing 13
IOC awards Helsinki, Finland 1940 Summer Olympic Games after Tokyo, Japan withdraws (Second Sino-Japanese War)
Dutch Nazi collaborator Meinoud Rost of Tonningen appointed as liquidation commissar for all Marxist organizations
Germany occupiers forbid Dutch Communist Party (CPN) in Netherlands
Yanks beat Tigers 12-6 in 17
1st detachment of Women's Army Auxiliary Corps, begin basic training
Barbados dismiss Trinidad for 16 in 69 minutes, Derek Sealy taking 8-8; West Indian cricket domestic first class record
Legion of Merit Medal authorized by US congress
Transport #8 departs Angers, France with 827 French Jews sent to Auschwitz Concentration camp; 19 survive until the war's end
Jacques Lusseyranis arrested by the Gestapo after being betrayed by a fellow member of the French resistance
Adolf Hitlersurvives an assassination attempt led by German army officer Claus Von Stauffenberg
British and Canadian troops occupy Hill 67/Ifs/Bras/Frenouville, Normandy
Browns Nelson Potter is first pitcher suspended for throwing spitballs
Canadian Cameron Highlanders conquer St Andre
Death March of 1,200 Jews from Lipcani Moldavia begins
Fieldmarshal von Kluge consults with German commandant at Caen
Fifty are hurt in rioting in front of the presidential palace in Mexico City
Flying Fortresses of US 8th Air Force attack Leipzig/Dessau
Heavy storm hampers British offensive at Caen
Japanese aircraft carrier Hijo sunk by US air attack
Liberators of US 8th Air Force attack Gotha Russelsheim/Eisenach
US 15th Air Force attacks Friedrichshaven Memmingen
US 9th Air Force bombs railroad at Chaulnes Sable-sur-Sarthe/Dreux
US invades Japanese-occupied Guam
Violent battles in Verrieres-hill (Normandy)
1st political action of Netherlands Army on Java and Sumatra
US Communist Party chairman William Forster arrested
Israel's 19 month war of independence ends
Vasil Kolarov elected Premier of Bulgaria
"Arthur Murray Party" premieres on ABC TV (later DuMont, CBS, NBC)
King Abdullah I of Jordan is assassinated by a Palestinian while attending Friday prayers in Jerusalem
Talal bin Abdullah (42) is coronated as King of Jordan, after the assassination of his father
Emile Zatopek runs Olympic Record 10K (29:17.0)
The United Nations Economic and Social Council votes to make UNICEF a permanent agency
USSR/Israel recover diplomatic relations
West German secret service head Otto John defects to East Germany (DDR)
Confirmation of the first detection of the neutrino by Clyde Cowan, Frederick Reines, F. B. Harrison, H. W. Kruse, and A. D. McGuire published in "Science" (Cowan–Reines neutrino experiment)
France recognizes Tunisian independence
Great Britain refuses to lend Egypt money to build a second Aswan Dam
US performs atmospheric nuclear Test at Bikini Island
King Hussein of Jordan breaks off diplomatic relations with UAR
1st submerged submarine to fire Polaris missile (George Washington)
The head of the Physics Department at the Israel Institute of Technology, Kurt Sitte, is arrested for espionage
French military forces break the Tunisian siege of Bizerte
Leslie Bricusse & Anthony Newley's musical "Stop The World - I Want To Get Off" premieres at Queen's Theater in London's West End
Dmitri Shostakovichcompletes his 13th Symphony
France and Tunisia recover diplomatic relations
17 African states & Madagascar sign peace treaty with EC
Riots break out at Mt Eden Prison, Auckland, New Zealand
Verne Gagne beats Crusher Lisowski in Minneapolis, to become NWA champ
1st surfin' record to go #1-Jan & Dean's "Surf City"
Dmitri Shostakovichcompletes his 10th String quartet
18.18 inches (46.18 cm) of rainfall in Edgarton, Missouri (state 24-hour record)
Race riots in Memphis, Tennessee
First Special Olympics for children and adults with intellectual disabilities is held in Chicago, Illinois
Iron Butterfly's "In-a-gadda-da-vida" becomes first heavy metal song to hit charts; it comes in at #117
Dodgers Bill Singer no-hits the Phillies 5-0, giving up no walks

Lucy the Elephant (née Elephant Bazaar), a six story building in the shape of an elephant built by a real estate speculator in 1881 is relocated two blocks south near the beach in Margate, New Jersey and refurbished as a roadside tourist attraction
US performs nuclear Test at Nevada Test Site
Chic's Wilbur Wood starts and loses both games of a doubleheader with NY Yankees, 12-2, & 7-0
The US Senate passes the War Powers Act
Heng Yo and Heng Ju, completes 1,000 mile (SF-Seattle) pilgrimage
Turkey invades Cyprus establishing Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus
Bruce Springteen and the E Street Band kick off the Born to Run Tour at the Palace Theatre in Providence, Rhode Island; Steven Van Zandt debuts as a full-fledged member of the group
India expels three reporters from "The Times", "The Daily Telegraph", and "Newsweek" because they refuse to sign a pledge to abide by government censorship
Vietnam War: The US military completes its troop withdrawal from Thailand
Viking 1 lands on Mars at Chryse Planitia, the first successful Martian landing
Flash flood hits Johnstown, Pennsylvania, kills 80 and causes $350 million damage
The Central Intelligence Agency releases documents under the Freedom of Information Act revealing it had engaged in mind control experiments
A 44 kg Newfoundland dog pulls a 2,293 kg load in Bothell, Washington
England set for innings loss v Aust, Botham hits 100 in 87 balls
Hyde Park and Regent's Park bombings: 11 British soldiers and 7 military horses killed in Provisional Irish Republican Army bomb attacks during military ceremonies in London
T Macauly & D Vosburghs musical "Windy City," premieres in London
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Uwe Hohn of East Germany throws javelin a record 104.8 m
Divers find wreck of Spanish galleon Atocha
The government of Aruba passes legislation to secede from the Netherlands Antilles
93°F, highest overnight low ever recorded in Phoenix, Arizona, until heat wave of 2023
Photographer Robert Mapplethorpe's show opens at Washington, D.C.'s Project for the Arts after the Smithsonian Institution's Corcoran Gallery cancels it
Justice William J. Brennan Jr. resigns from the Supreme Court after 36 years
Round World Air Race begins in Paris
Fire in the press box at Atlanta Fulton County Stadium
Major parts of Comet Shoemaker-Levy hit Jupiter (July 16th-22nd)
The Regents of the University of California vote to end all affirmative action in the UC system by 1997.
In Spain an ETA bomb at an airport kills 35
32.52" (82.6 cm) of rainfall, Dauphin Island, Alabama (started on the 19th); new state record
Two hundred aid workers from CARE International, Médecins Sans Frontières (Doctors Without Borders) and other aid groups leave Afghanistan on orders of the Taliban
Falun Gong is banned in the People's Republic of China, and a large scale crackdown of the practice is launched
In Zimbabwe, Parliament opens its new session and seats opposition members for the first time in a decade
Terrorist Carlos the Jackal sues France in the European Court of Human Rights for allegedly torturing him
The leaders of Salt Lake City's bid to win the 2002 Winter Olympics are indicted by a federal grand jury for bribery, fraud, and racketeering
The 27th Annual G8 summit opens in Genoa, Italy. An Italian protester in Genoa, Carlo Giuliani, is shot by police.
The London Stock Exchange goes public
Bartenders doing tricks with fire start a major fire in a night club in Lima, Peru that kills 25 and injures 100
France: Sixteen people are injured after two bombs explode outside a tax office in Nice.
Canada becomes the fourth country in the world to legalize same-sex marriage, after the bill C-38 receives its Royal Assent
12 people are killed and 59 injured after a gunman opens fire at a Dark Knight movie premier in Aurora, Colorado,
21 people are killed and 29 injured in a bus accident in Nayarit, Mexico
Isaac the Jew, diplomat to Carolingian EmperorCharlemagnereturns to Aachen from court of Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid with famous gifts, including a water clock and Abul Abbas the elephant
Wars of Scottish Independence: Fall of Stirling Castle - KingEdward Iof England takes the last rebel stronghold of the war
Battle of Ankara: Army ofTimur(Tamerlane) with its armor-plated elephants defeats forces of Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I, capturing Bayezid in the process leading to an Ottoman power vacuum
Holy RomanEmperor Maximilian Inames Albert III, Duke of Saxony the Governor of Friesland
Scottish explorerAlexander Mackenziecompletes the first European east-to-west crossing of America north of Mexico
Napoleon Bonapartegrants a patent for a Pyréolophore, an early combustion engine for a boat, to brothersNicéphoreandClaude Niépce
Napoleondecrees all French Jews must adopt a fixed family name
Gathering of Plombieres: French EmperorNapoleon IIImeets the Prime Minister of Piedmont-Sardinia, Count Cavour
Committee finds Former Dutch Prime MinisterAbraham Kuyperinnocent of corruption in the so-called decorations affairs
NY Giants trade future Baseball HOF pitcherChristy Mathewsonto Cincinnati Reds
CongresswomanAlice Mary Robertsonbecomes the first woman to preside over the floor of US House of Representatives
An injuredBabe Ruthentertains the touring Australian cricketers in his private box at Yankee Stadium as his NY Yankees beat the Chicago White Sox 7-2; 'The Babe' meets 'The Don' (Don Bradman)
Franz von Papenlaunches a coup against the Prussian government
Vatican state secretary Pacelli (Pius XII) signs the Reichskonkordat accord withHitler's Nazi Germany
The Guinea Pig Club forms, made of severely injured airmen treated byArchibald McIndoeat Queen Victoria Hospital, East Grinstead, England
Time magazine features Russian composerDmitri Shostakovichon its cover
US Joint Chiefs of Staff question AdmiralChester Nimitzover the landing on Gilbert Island
GeneralEisenhowervisits Montgomery's headquarter in Normandy
US PresidentFDRnominated for an unprecedented fourth term at Democratic convention
Lou Theszbeats Bill Longson, to become NWA wrestling champion
Syngman Rheeelected the 1st President of South Korea
American tennis championMaureen Connolly's right leg is crushed in a horse riding accident ending a brilliant career at just 19
Yankee pitcherWhitey Fordties AL record of 6 straight strike-outs
Detroit Tigers future Baseball HOF pitcherJim Bunningno-hits Boston Red Sox, 3-0
Columbia records releaseBob Dylansingle "Like a Rolling Stone"
NY Yankee pitcherMel Stottlemyrehits an inside-the-park grand slam at Yankee Stadium in 6-3 win over the Boston Red Sox
Chilean poet and diplomatPablo Nerudareceives 1st Viareggio-Versilia Prize, recognizing “world personalities who work for culture and understanding between peoples”
Jane Asher breaks her engagement with Beatle bassist, singer, and songwriterPaul McCartney, live on BBC-TV's "Dee Time" program
Judy Garlandheadlines concert also featuringCount BasieandJackie Wilsonat JFK Stadium, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania; her last US concert
Jack Briscobeats Harley Race in Houston, to become NWA champ
Hank Aaronhits his 755th and last home run off Angels pitcher Dick Drago
New York YankeesDon Mattinglyties 1st base fielding record with 22 put-outs
Michael Dukakisselected Democratic presidential nominee
Burma government puts authorAung San Suu Kyiunder house arrest
Mike Tysonis accused of raping a Miss Black America contestant
Václav Havelresigns as President of Czechoslovakia after Slovakia declares independence
Alexander Lukashenkois inaugurated as the first president of Belarus
Former NFL running back, broadcaster and actorO.J. Simpsonoffers $500,000 reward for evidence of ex-wife's klller
Supreme Leader of North-Korea,Kim Il-sungis placed in a public Mausoleum at the Kumsusan Palace of the Sun
"Spirited Away," written and directed byHayao Miyazaki, is released in Japan (Academy Award Best Animated Feature 2003)
During an appearance on "The Tonight Show",Colin Farrellis confronted by telephone sex worker Dessarae Bradford, who had been stalking the actor
16th ESPY Awards:Tiger Woods, Candace Parker win
Anglo-Portuguese treaty is reinforced, England guarantees Portugal’s independence and receives trade concessions
Euston railway station opens in London as the terminus of the London and Birmingham Railway (L&BR), the city's first intercity railway station

NASA's Apollo 11 lunar module, carryingNeil Armstrongand Buzz Aldrin, lands on the surface of the Moon. Seven hours later, Armstrong becomes the first person to step on the moon at 10:56 pm EDT, and Aldrin joins him shortly after. Michael Collins remains in orbit in the command module.
Imam Al-Bukhari [Muḥammad al-Bukhari], Islamic scholar and compiler of Hadith (Sahih al-Bukhari), born in Bukhara, Abbasid Caliphate (d. 870)
Francesco Petrarch, Italian poet (Italia Mia), born in Arezzo, Tuscany (d. 1374)
Gaspar Schetz, Dutch nobleman and financier (royal treasurer general of the Habsburg Netherlands), born in Antwerp, Duchy of Brabant, Holy Roman Empire (now Belgium) (d. 1580)
Pope Innocent IX [Giovanni Antonio Facchinetti], 230th Catholic pope (1591), born in Bologna, Papal States (now Italy) (d. 1591)
Arnaud d'Ossat, French diplomat and writer, born in Larroque-Magnoac, France (d. 1604)
Anne Hutchison, English religious reformer and key figure in the Massachusetts Bay Colony (Antinomian Controversy), born in Alford, Lincolnshire, England (d. 1643) [date of baptism]
Simon Ives, English organist, lyra-violist, and court composer (King Charles I), baptized in Ware, Herfordshire, England (d. 1662)
Johann Bernhard Fischer von Erlach, Austrian Baroque architect under the Habsburgs (Schönbrunn Palace), born in Graz (d. 1723)
Hyacinthe Rigaud, French painter, born in Perpignan, France (d. 1743)
Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville, French Canadian sailor, explorer and adventurer (Hudson Bay, Louisiana), born in Montréal, New France (d. 1706)
Pierre Drevet, French engraver, born in Loire-sur-Rhône, Lyon (d. 1738)
James Harris, English philosopher, born in Salisbury, Wiltshire (d. 1780)
William Jones, British composer and clergyman, born in Lowick, England (d. 1800)
Henri Hamal, Belgian organist and composer, born in Liège, Prince-Bishopric of Liège, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1820)
Antoine Destutt de Tracy, French Enlightenment philosopher, born in Paris (d. 1836)
Garsevan Chavchavadze, Georgian diplomat and politician, born in Georgia (d. 1811)
Joseph Lefèbvre, French composer, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1822?)
Jakob Haibel, Austrian composer, born in Graz, Austria (d. 1826)
Thomas Bruce, 7th earl of Elgin & Kincardine, British diplomat, born in Broomhall, Fife, Scotland (d. 1841)
Auguste Marmont, French marshal, born in Châtillon-sur-Seine, France (d. 1852)
Ignaz Schuster, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1835)
Aleksander Fredro, Polish poet and comedy writer (Pan Jowiolski), born in Surochów, Poland (d. 1876)
Edward Hodges, English organist and composer, born in Bristol, England (d. 1867)
Paweł Edmund Strzelecki, Polish explorer and geologist, born in Glausche, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1873)
Jakob Zeugheer, Swiss composer, born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1865)
Richard Owen, English zoologist (Dinosauria), born in Lancaster, England (d. 1892)
John Sterling, Scottish writer, born in Isle of Bute, United Kingdom (d. 1844)
James Bruce, 8th Earl of Elgin & Kincardine and Governor (Jamaica), born in London (d. 1863)
William Bowman, English anatomist, born in Nantwich, Cheshire (d. 1892)
Paul Henrion, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1901)
Alexander Schimmelfennig, Prussian-American political revolutionary and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Bromberg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1865)
Thomas Allbutt, English physiologist (Diseases of the Heart), born in Dewsbury, Yorkshire (d. 1925)
Hans Sommer (né Zincke), German theatre composer, born in Braunschweig, Duchy of Brunswick, German Confederation (d. 1922)
Augustin Daly, American playwright and theatrical manager, born in Plymouth, North Carolina (d. 1899)
George Otto Trevelyan, British statesman and biographer, born in Rothley Temple, England (d. 1928)
Max Liebermann, German impressionist painter and print maker, born in Berlin, Prussia (d. 1935)
Robert Anderson Van Wyck, American first Mayor of New York City, born in New York City (d. 1918)
John G. Shedd, American businessman (President of Marshall Field & Company, 1906-22), civic leader, and philanthropist (Shedd Aquarium), born in Alstead, New Hampshire (d. 1926)
Theo Heemskerk, Dutch lawyer, and politician (Prime Minister, 1908-13; Council of State, 1913-18; Minister of Justice, 1918-25), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands  (d. 1932)
George Poggenbeek, Dutch painter, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1903)
Paul Viardot, French violinist and composer, born in Courtavenel, France (d. 1941)
Juan Vucetich, Croatian-born Argentine anthropologist who pioneered fingerprint identification, born in Hvar, Kingdom of Dalmatia (d. 1925)
Erik Axel Karlfeldt, Swedish poet (Nobel Prize for Literature 1919-refused; 1931-posthumous), born in Karlbo, Sweden (d. 1931)[1]
Miron Cristea, 1st Patriarch of All Romania, Prime Minister of Romania (1938-39), born in Toplița, Romania (d. 1939)
Vladimir Dmitrievich Nabokov, Russian politician and criminologist (Minister of Justice 1918-19) father of the writer, born in Tsarskoe Selo, Russia (d. 1922)
Alexander "Alick" Maclean, British composer and conductor (Scarborough Spa Orchestra, 1912-35), born in Windsor, Berkshire, England (d. 1936)[1]
Déodat de Séverac, French composer (Cerdaña), born in Saint-Félix-de-Caraman, France (d. 1921)
Alberto Santos-Dumont, Brazilian balloonist, airship builder, and aviator (1st plane flight in Europe), born in Palmira, Empire of Brazil (d. 1932)
Witold Maliszewski, Polish composer (founder and first director of Odessa Conservatory), born in Mohyliv-Podilskyi, Russian Empire (now Ukraine) (d. 1939)
Otto Blumenthal, German mathematician, journal editor (Mathematische Annalen, 1906-38) and university professor, born in Frankfurt, Kingdom of Prussia (now Germany) (d. 1944)
Percy Dean, British naval lieutenant and politician who received the Victoria Cross for saving men during the raid of Zeebrugge, born in Blackburn, Lancashire, England (d. 1939)
Hermann von Keyserling, Baltic German social philosopher and writer, born in Könno, Governorate of Livonia, Russian Empire (d. 1946) [OS 8 July]
Leon de Smet, Belgian painter (Luminist school), born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1966)
Gustave Charlier, Belgian literary historian and critic, born in Huy, Belgium (d. 1959)
John Charles Reith, 1st Baron Reith, British broadcasting executive and 1st director-general of BBC (1927-38), born in Stonehaven, Kincardineshire, Scotland (d. 1971)
Verna Felton, American actress (Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Lady and the Tramp, December Bride), born in Salinas, California (d. 1966)
George Llewelyn-Davies, English inspiration for the Peter Pan character, born in London (d. 1915)
Richard Billinger, Austrian poet and writer (From Where I Came), born in Sankt Marienkirchen, Austria (d. 1965)
László Moholy-Nagy, Hungarian painter, photographer, and sculptor, born in Borsód, Austria-Hungary (d. 1946)
Tadeusz Reichstein, Polish-Swiss chemist (Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine 1950 - isolation and discovery of cortisone ), born in Wloclawek, Poland (d. 1996)[1]
Gottfried von Haberler, Austrian-American economist (international trade), born in Purkersdorf, Austria-Hungary (d. 1995)
Maurice Gilliams, Belgian writer and poet (Maria's Life), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1982)
Maurice Leyland, English cricket batsman (41 Tests, 2764 runs @ 46.06; Yorkshire 1920-46, 1,000+ runs in 17 consecutive seasons), born in Harrogate, England (d. 1967)
Heinie Manush, American Baseball HOF left fielder (MLB All-Star 1934 Washington Sens; AL batting champion 1926 Detroit Tigers), born in Tuscumbia, Alabama (d. 1971)
Vehbi Koç, Turkish industrialist (Koç Group)and philanthropist, born in Ankara, Turkey (d. 1996)
Dilys Powell, English film critic and travel writer (Descent from Parnassus), born in Bridgnorth, England (d. 1995)
Jimmy Kennedy, Irish singer-songwriter ("Teddy Bears' Picnic"; "My Prayer"), born in Omagh, Ireland (d. 1984)
Jef van de Wiele, Belgian pro-Nazi politician, born in Deume, Belgium (d. 1979)
Molly Keane, Irish writer, and playwright, born in Newbridge, Ireland (d. 1996)
Gunnar de Frumerie, Swedish pianist and composer (Circulus Quintus), born in Nacka, Sweden (d. 1987)
Eric Rowan, South African cricket batsman (26 Tests, 3 x 100, HS 236; Transvaal, Eastern Province), born in Johannesburg, South Africa (d. 1993)
Jean Focas, Greco-French astronomer, created map of Mars, born in Corfu (d. 1969)
Cicely Veronica Wedgwood [C. V. Wedgwood], British historian (William the Silent), born in Stocksfield, England (d. 1997)
Vilém Tauský, Czech conductor (BBC Concert Orchestra, 1956-66), opera director and educator (Guildhall School of Music and Drama, 1966-92), and composer, born in Prerov, Moravia (d. 2004)
William Dillard, American jazz trumpeter and Broadway singer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1995)[1]
Frederick Ferrari, English tenor and variety performer (Love Descended Like An Angel), born in Manchester, England (d. 1994)
George Johnston, Australian journalist and novelist "My Brother Jack", born in Melbourne, Victoria (d. 1970)
John Vivian Dacie, English haematologist (discovered haemophilia B), born in London, England (d. 2005)
William John Phillips, British actor (Nothing but a Man, Z-Cars), born in Birmingham, Warwickshire, England (d. 1995)
Cindy Walker, American songwriter ("Dream Baby"; "You Don't Know Me") and country singer, born in Mart, Texas (d. 2006)

Elliot Richardson, American attorney and administrator (Secretary of Defense, 1973; Attorney General, 1973; Ambassador to UK, 1975-76; Secretary of Commerce, 1976-77), and WWII veteran, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1999)
Ted Schroeder, American tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1942, Wimbledon 1949), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 2006)
Alan S. Boyd, American politician and transportation executive (1st US Secretary of Transportation), born in Jacksonville, Florida (d. 2020)
Ernie Wilkins, American jazz saxophonist, arranger (Count Basie, Harry James), and bandleader, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1999)
Karel Krautgartner, Czech jazz clarinetist, saxophonist, arranger, composer, conductor, and teacher, born in Mikulov, Moravia (d. 1982)
Stanisław Albinowski, Polish economist and journalist, born in Lwów (d. 2005)
Hans Lodeizen [Johannes Frederik], Dutch poet (Het innerlijk behang (The Inner Wallpaper)), born in Naarden, Netherlands (d. 1950)
Lola Albright, American singer and actress (Delta Country; Kid Galahad; A Cold Wind in August), born in Akron, Ohio (d. 2017)
Mort Garson, Canadian-American composer ("Our Day Will Come") and Moog synthesizer pioneer, born in St. John, New Brunswick (d. 2008)
Robert D. Maurer, American scientist and co-inventor of optical fiber, born in St. Louis, Missouri
Thomas Berger, American novelist (Vital Parts, Little Big Man), born in Cincinnati, Ohio, (d. 2014)
Frantz Fanon, West Indian psychiatrist and philosopher (The Wretched of the Earth), born in Martinique, France (d. 1961)
Jacques Delors, French economist and politician (President of the European Commission, 1985-95; Minister of Finance, 1981-84; Member of European Parliament. 1979-81), born in Paris, France (d. 2023)
Patricia "Pat" Cutts, British actress (Coronation Street), born in London, England (d. 1974)
Anthony Cavendish, British MI 6 officer and banker, born in London (d. 2013)
Lyudmila Alexeyeva, Russian 'Grandmother of Russian Human Rights', born in Crimea, Russia (d. 2018)
Michael Gielen, Austrian conductor (Frankfurt Opera, 1967-87); Cincinnati Symphony, 1980-86); Southwest German Radio Symphony, 1986-99), and composer, born in Dresden, Germany (d. 2019)
Charles David Ganao, Prime Minister of the Republic of the Congo (1996-1997), born in Djambala, French Equatorial Africa (d. 2012)
Jan Meyers, American politician (Rep-R-Kansas, 1985-97), born in Lincoln, Nebraska (d. 2019)
Pavel Kohout, Czech-Austrian playwright (Poor Murderer), poet, novelist (I Am Snowing), and dissident (Charter 77), born in Prague, Czechoslovakia
Peter Ind, British jazz double bassist, born in Middlesex, England (d. 2021)
Rajendra Kumar, Indian actor, producer and director (Arzoo, Love Story, Sangam), born in Sialkot, Punjab, British India (d. 1999)
Chuck Daly, American Basketball Hall of Fame coach (2-time NBA champion Detroit Pistons), born in Kane, Pennsylvania (d. 2009)
Donald Moffitt, American sci-fi author (The Jupiter Theft; The Genesis Quest), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2014)[1]
Phil Coles, Australian sports administrator (International Olympic Committee 1982-2011; AOC 1973-2011), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 2023)
Tony Marsh, English auto racer (record 6 x British Hill Climb Championship), born in Stourbridge, England (d. 2009)
Dan M. Rooney, American football executive and diplomat, son of the Steelers' founder Art Rooney (Pittsburgh Steelers Hall of Honor), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2017)
